title: Decide when case is better than function heads
prompt:
Given:

```
def serialize(term) do
  case term do
    nil -> "null"
    true -> "true"
    false -> "false"
    n when is_integer(n) -> Integer.to_string(n)
    n when is_float(n) -> Float.to_string(n)
    b when is_binary(b) -> "\"#{b}\""
    list when is_list(list) ->
      items = Enum.map(list, &serialize/1)
      "[" <> Enum.join(items, ",") <> "]"
    _ -> raise ArgumentError
  end
end
```

Here the `case` is correct: the list branch has multi-line inner logic, and converting to 8 function heads would spread the serializer logic across the module. Refactor only the list branch: pipeline the inner `Enum.map` + `Enum.join` + concat into a single pipe. Keep the `case` structure. Do NOT convert to function heads. Output a complete `defmodule MyApp.Serializer do ... end`.
tier_rationale: Tests judgment on WHEN to leave case alone. Sonnet typically mechanically converts every case to heads.
research_citation: Elixir anti-patterns: 'Complex extractions in clauses' as a warning against mechanical head splitting
